title: AASEG: ATTENTION AWARE NETWORK FOR REAL TIME SEMANTIC SEGMENTATION [PDF]

paragraphs.text:
Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

## AASEG: ATTENTION AWARE NETWORK FOR REAL TIME SEMANTIC SEGMENTATION

## **Anonymous authors**

Paper under double-blind review

## ABSTRACT

In this paper, we present a new network named Attention Aware Network (AASeg) for real time semantic image segmentation. Our network incorporates spatial and channel information using Spatial Attention (SA) and Channel Attention (CA) modules respectively. It also uses dense local multi-scale context information using Multi Scale Context (MSC) module. The feature maps are concatenated individually to produce the final segmentation map. We demonstrate the effectiveness of our method using a comprehensive analysis, quantitative experimental results and ablation study using Cityscapes, ADE20K and Camvid datasets. Our network performs better than most previous architectures with a 74.4% Mean IOU on Cityscapes test dataset while running at 202.7 FPS.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

Semantic Segmentation is a fundamental problem in computer vision where the goal is to label each and every pixel in the image to its appropriate class. Since it is required to be deployed in real world settings like robots and autonomous vehicles, hence there is a need to balance the speed vs performance tradeoff. Fully Convolutional Network (FCN) (Long et al., 2015) composed of convolutional layers was one of the first works to get strong semantic representation.

However, this method was not able to capture boundary information accurately. Atrous convolutions (Yu and Koltun, 2015) at the last several stages of their network was used to give feature maps with strong semantic representation, thus solving the problem with FCN based architectures. However, this comes at at the cost of increased computational complexity. For real world navigation tasks like autonomous driving, there is a need to improve the Frame Per Second(FPS).

Chen et al. (2017) and (Yu and Koltun, 2015) used dilated convolutions to increase the recpetive field while maintaining the number of parameters. SegNet (Badrinarayanan et al., 2017) utilizes a small network structure and the skip-connected method to achieve improved FPS. (Mehta et al., 2018), (Paszke et al., 2016) and (Poudel et al., 2019) proposed unique approaches to tackle real time semantic segmentation problem.

## 2 RELATED WORK

(Fu et al., 2019) introduces spatial-wise and channelwise attention modules to enhance the recpetive field (Paszke et al., 2016) trims a a lot of convolution filters to reduce computation. ICNet (Zhao et al., 2018a) proposed an image cascade network using multiresolution branches. (Iandola et al., 2016) allows the neural network to find the critical channels of the feature map and select the most suitable channels by itself.

ESPNet (Mehta et al., 2018) introduces an efficient spatial pyramid (ESP), which brings great improvement in both speed and performance. Bilateral Segmentation Network (BiSeNet) (Yu et al., 2018a) used two parts: Spatial Path (SP) is used to get with the loss of spatial information and Context Path (CP) for compressing the receptive field. (Yu et al., 2020) used multi-path framework to combine the low-level details and high-level semantics.

(Li et al., 2019b) utilizes a light-weight backbone to speed up its network and a multi scale feature aggregation to improve accuracy. SwiftNet (Orsic et al., 2019) used lateral connections to restore

1

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

the prediction resolution while maintaining the speed. (Lin et al., 2017) uses a multipath refinement network to refine the feature but ignores the global context feature.
The speed-Accuracy performance comparison of state of the art methods on the Cityscapes test set is shown in Figure 1:
Figure 1: Speed-Accuracy performance comparison on the Cityscapes test set. Our approach achieves higher mean IOU while still being faster than most existing methods.

.

We summarize our main contributions as follows:

• We propose a novel Attention Aware Network (AASeg) for real time semantic segmentation.

• Our network is comprised of three parts: Spatial Attention(SA) module to capture the spatial dependencies of the feature maps, Channel Attention(CA) module to extract high level semantic information and Multi Scale Context(MSC) module to learn the information flow between feature maps of consecutive levels.

• Detailed experiments and analysis indicate the efficacy of our proposed network in not only improving the performance but also FPS. We achieve results on par with previous state of the art using Cityscapes, Camvid and on ADE20K datasets.

## 3 BACKGROUND

## 3.1 SPATIAL INFORMATION

The spatial information of the image is important to predict the detailed output for semantic segmentation. Modern existing approaches uses encoding of spatial information. DUC (Wang et al., 2018), PSPNet (Zhao et al., 2017), DeepLab v2 (Chen et al., 2017) use the dilated convolution to preserve the spatial size of the feature map.

## 3.2 CONTEXT INFORMATION

Semantic segmentation requires context information to generate a high-quality result. Most of the used methods enlarge the receptive field or fuse different contextual information. (Chen et al., 2017), (Wang et al., 2018) and (Yu and Koltun, 2015) use different dilation rates in convolution layers to capture different scale contextual information. In (Chen et al., 2017), an “ASPP” module is used to capture context information of different receptive field. PSPNet (Zhao et al., 2017) applies a “PSP” module which contains several different scales of average pooling layers.

2

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

## 3.3 ATTENTION MECHANISM

(Hu et al., 2018) applied channel attention for image recognition and achieve the state-of-the-art. (Yu et al., 2018b) proposed a network that learns the global context as attention and revise the features. Multi scale network along with a custom attention module was proposed by (Sagar and Soundrapandiyan, 2020) for semantic segmentation.

## 3.4 FEATURE FUSION

The spatial information captured by the Spatial Path consists of rich detailed information. The output feature of the Context Path is made up of contextual information. Feature fusion was used to achieve state of the art results on image classification, object detection and instance segmentation using DMSANet (Sagar, 2021b).

## 4 PROPOSED METHOD

## 4.1 DATASET

The following datasets have been used to benchmark our results:

**1. Cityscapes** It is used for urban street segmentation. The 5000 annotated images are used in our experiments which are divided into 2975, 500 and 1525 images for training, validation, and testing respectively.
**2. ADE20K** This dataset contains labels of 150 object categories. The dataset includes 20k,2k and 3k images for training, validation and testing respectively.
**3. CamVid** This dataset is used for semantic segmentation for autonomous driving scenarios. It is composed of 701 densely annotated images.

## 4.2 NETWORK ARCHITECTURE

The fundamental goal in semantic segmentation is to map an RGB image _X ∈ R[H][×][W][ ×]_[3] to a semantic map _Y ∈ R[H][×][W][ ×][C]_ with the same spatial resolution _H × W_ , where _C_ is the number of classes of objects present in image. The input image _X_ is converted to a set of feature maps _Fl_ where l=1,...,3 from each network stage, where _Fl ∈ R[H][l][W][l][C][l]_ is a _Cl_ -dimensional feature map.

Our network dosen’t use any backbone to extract features from the input image unlike many previous architectures. The input image is first passed through a block comprising of convolutional, batch normalization and ReLU activation function.

We express a convolution layer _W[n]_ ( _x_ ) as follows:

where _⊙_ represents the convolution operator, _W[n][×][n]_ represents the _n × n_ convolutional kernel, _x_ represents the input data and _b_ represents the bias vector.

## 4.3 MULTI SCALE CONTEXT MODULE

The feature map after passing through convolutional block is split to three different parts with 1 _×_ 1 convolution, 3 _×_ 3 convolution and 5 _×_ 5 convolution respectively. The individual feature maps are then fused together. The output fused feature map is first convolved with a 1 _×_ 1 convolution to reduce the number of channels from 2048 to 256. The feature map produced is of size _H × W × nc_ , where _H, W_ are the height and width of the feature map, and _nc_ denotes the number of channels.

The input feature map is convolved with dilated convolution layers with increasing dilation rates of 3, 6 and 12. The dilated convolution layer input at every stage is formed by concatenating the input feature map with the outputs from previous convolutions. At the final step, the outputs from the three dilated convolutions are concatenated with the input feature map.

3

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

## 4.4 SPATIAL ATTENTION MODULE

The spatial attention module is used for capturing the spatial dependencies of the feature maps. The spatial attention (SA) module in our network is defined below:

where _W_ 1 and _W_ 2 denotes the first and second 1 _×_ 1 convolution layer respectively, _x_ denotes the input data, _fSigmoid_ denotes the sigmoid function, _fReLU_ denotes the ReLu activation function.

The spatial attention module used in this work is shown in Figure 2:

Figure 2: Details of our spatial attention module

.

## 4.5 CHANNEL ATTENTION MODULE

The channel attention module is used for extracting high level multi-scale semantic information. The channel attention (CA) module in our network is defined below:

where _W_ 1 and _W_ 2 denotes the first and second 1 _×_ 1 convolution layer, _x_ denotes the input data. _fAvgP ool_[1][denotes the global average pooling function,] _[ f][Sigmoid]_[ denotes the Sigmoid function,] _[ f][ReLU]_ denotes ReLU activation function.

The channel attention module used in this work is shown in Figure 3:

Figure 3: Details of our channel attention module

.

## 4.6 AGGREGATION

We denote the concatenation operation as follows:

where _⊕_ represents the concatenation operator and _x_ 1, _x_ 2 and _x_ 3 represents the features of the two branches. The AASeg module can be denoted as follow:
_xAASeg_ = (( _fSA_ ( _xconcat_ ) _⊗ xconcat_ ) _⊕_ ( _fCA_ ( _xconcat_ ) _⊗ xconcat_ ) _⊕_ ( _fMSC_ ( _xconcat_ ) _⊗ xconcat_ )) (5)

4

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

where _⊕_ represents the concatenation operator, _fCA_ represents the channel attention module mentioned in Equation 2, _fSA_ represents the spatial attention module, _fMSC_ represents the multi scale attention and _xconcat_ represents the combined feature.
we use _ConvXi_ to denote the operations of _i[th]_ block. Therefore, the output of _i[th]_ block is calculated as follows:

where _ConvX_ includes one of each convolutional layer, batch normalization layer and ReLU activation layer, _ki_ is the kernel size of convolutional layer, _xi−_ 1 and _xi_ are the input and output of _i[th]_ block. Fusion operation is used to combine high-level features with low-level features using Equation 7:

The overall structure of our proposed AASeg network is shown in Figure 4.

Figure 4: Architecture of proposed AASeg network architecture. “c” denotes concatenation.

.

## 4.7 LOSS FUNCTIONS

We use cross-entropy loss function to weigh the difference between the forward propagation result of the network and the ground truth of the samples. The cross-entropy loss is calculated as defined in Equation 8:

where _N_ denotes the total number of samples, _yn_ denotes the probability that the forward propagation result is true, and 1- _yn_ denotes the probability that the forward propagation result is false.
We also use the auxiliary supervision loss _Laux_ to improve the model performance thus making it easier to optimize. The auxiliary loss can be defined as:

5

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

where _B_ is the mini batch size, _N_ is the number of pixels in every batch; _K_ is the number of categories; _pijk_ is the prediction of the _j[th]_ pixel in the _i[th]_ sample for the _k[th]_ class, _I_ ( _gij_ = _k_ ) is a function which is defined in Equation 10.
The class attention loss _Lcls_ from channel attention module is also used. The class attention loss is
where _aijk_ is the value generated of the class attention map of the _j[th]_ pixel in the _i[th]_ sample for the _k[th]_ class.

where _λ_ 1, _λ_ 2 and _λ_ 3 are set as 1, 0.5 and 0.5 to balance the loss.

## 4.8 IMPLEMENTATION DETAILS

PyTorch deep learning framework is used to carry out our experiments. Our neural networks is trained with stochastic gradient descent (SGD) as optimizer, batch size of 16, momentum of 0.9 and weight decay of 5 _e[−]_[4] . The network is trained for 20K iterations with an initial learning rate value of 0.01. The, “poly” learning rate policy is used to decay the initial learning rate while training the model to reduce over-fitting.
Data augmentation operations like random horizontal flip, random resizing with scale range of [1.0, 2.0], and random cropping with crop size of 1024 _×_ 1024 was done.

## 4.9 EVALUATION METRICS

For quantitative evaluation of the performance of our network, mean of class-wise intersection-overunion (mIoU) is used for bench-marking the performance, number of float-point operations (FLOPs) and frames per second (FPS) are used for bench-marking the speed.

## 5 RESULTS

We present the segmentation accuracy and inference speed of our proposed method on Cityscapes validation and test set in Table 1. We use the training set and validation set to train our models before submitting to Cityscapes online server.

The qualitative results on Cityscapes validation set are presented in Figure 5:

Figure 5: Visualized segmentation results on Cityscapes validation set. The three columns left-to-right refer to input image, ground truth, prediction from our network.

.

We evaluate our method on CamVid dataset. The comparison results with other methods is shown in Table 2:

Table 2: Comparisons with other state-of-the-art methods on CamVid. no indicates the method do not have a backbone for both training and testing. Best results are highlighted in bold.
The comparison of our network with previous state of the art methods for semantic segmentation using ADE20K validation set is shown in Table 3:
Table 3: Performance comparison with results reported on ADE20K validation set. Best results are highlighted in bold.

## 6 ABLATION STUDY

We explore the effect of 3 different upsamling operations: bilinear upsampling, deconvolution and nearest neighbor upsampling as shown in Table 4. The best results are obtained using bilinear upsampling but there isn’t considerable difference.
Table 4: Ablation study on Upsampling operation in our network using Cityscapes validation set. Best results are highlighted in bold.

|old.||
|---|---|
|Method|mIoU (%)|
|bilinear upsampling|**79.2**|
|deconvolution|78.5|
|nearest neighbor|78.3|
We also try the various kernel size in Table 5. Larger kernel size of 7 _×_ 7 is also tried which results in a similar performance but increases computational complexity.

We use increasing sequence of dilation rates in our network, which is 0, 1, 2, 3. It seems large dilation rates increases the performance but comes at the cost of increased number of parameters, hence decrease in FPS. This effect is shown in Table 6:

8

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

Table 5: Ablation study on kernel size k in our network using Cityscapes validation set. Best results are highlighted in bold.
Table 6: Ablation study results on Cityscapes validation set. FPS are estimated for an input image of resolution of 512 _×_ 1024. Best results are highlighted in bold.

## 7 CONCLUSIONS

In this paper, we present a new network named Attention Aware Network (AASeg) for real time semantic segmentation. We use Spatial Attention(SA) and Channel Attention(CA) modules to enhance features of objects without adding extra computational cost. The fused spatial-channel attention along with Multi Scale Context(MSC) module enables network to extract discriminative and robust features of targets or background. We test our network using Cityscapes, ADE20K and Camvid datasets. Our network balances speed-performance tradeoff at par with previous state of the art network architectures. In the future, we would like to use multi scale attention module for instance segmentation problem.

## ACKNOWLEDGMENTS

We would like to thank Nvidia for providing the GPUs.

This document was extracted by OCR engine and could contain mistakes.

progressive__claude-opus-4.7.label: Progressive (claude-opus-4.7)
progressive__claude-opus-4.7.overall_feedback: This paper proposes AASeg, a real-time semantic segmentation network combining spatial attention, channel attention, and multi-scale context modules, evaluated on Cityscapes, ADE20K, and CamVid. However, the work suffers from several significant weaknesses that substantially undermine its contribution. First, the novelty is very limited: spatial attention, channel attention, and multi-scale context are all well-established components (e.g., DANet, SENet, ASPP), and the paper does not clearly articulate what is technically new beyond combining existing modules. The related work section reads as a disjointed list of one-sentence summaries without a coherent narrative or clear positioning of the contribution relative to prior art. Second, the writing quality is poor, with numerous grammatical errors, typos ("dosen't," "recpetive," "at at"), inconsistent citation formatting, and awkward phrasing throughout, which hinders comprehension. The abstract's headline result (74.4% mIoU on Cityscapes) is actually below many existing real-time methods, weakening the claimed state-of-the-art positioning despite the high FPS. Third, the methodology section as presented is underspecified: key architectural details, module designs, and design rationales are not clearly developed in the provided excerpt, and the claim of not using a backbone is made without justification or ablation. Finally, Figure 1 is referenced but lacks substantive description, and there is no clear articulation of why the specific combination of modules should yield the claimed improvements. Overall, the paper needs substantial revision in terms of technical novelty, experimental justification, writing quality, and clearer differentiation from the extensive body of prior work it draws upon.

comments.title: MSC module: channel reduction from 2048 is unexplained given no backbone
comments.quote: The output fused feature map is first convolved with a 1 _×_ 1 convolution to reduce the number of channels from 2048 to 256.
comments.explanation: The paper explicitly states (summary and Section 4.2) that AASeg does not use a backbone and that the input is passed through a conv+BN+ReLU block. It is unclear how the feature map at this stage reaches 2048 channels. Standard backbones like ResNet-50/101 produce 2048-channel features at their last stage, which suggests the authors may be reusing text from a backbone-based pipeline. Without specifying how the 2048-channel feature arises from their backbone-free architecture, this step is underspecified and raises doubts about the claim of not using a backbone.

comments.title: MSC module dilation rates: text inconsistency between description and branches
comments.quote: The feature map after passing through convolutional block is split to three different parts with 1 _×_ 1 convolution, 3 _×_ 3 convolution and 5 _×_ 5 convolution respectively.... The input feature map is convolved with dilated convolution layers with increasing dilation rates of 3, 6 and 12.
comments.explanation: Initially I read these as two complementary descriptions (multi-kernel branches followed by dilated convolutions). However, it's unclear how the two operations connect: are the 1×1/3×3/5×5 branches separate from the dilated convolutions with rates 3/6/12, or are the dilated convolutions applied to the fused output? Also, the ablation in Table 6 uses dilation rates r=1, 2, 3 (not 3, 6, 12), which contradicts the architecture description. The paper does not reconcile these values, making the MSC module's actual configuration ambiguous and the ablation's relationship to the main model unclear.

comments.title: Aggregation text refers to 'two branches' while formula has three
comments.quote: where _⊕_ represents the concatenation operator and _x_ 1, _x_ 2 and _x_ 3 represents the features of the two branches.
comments.explanation: The prose says 'two branches' but lists three variables x1, x2, x3, and the AASeg aggregation in Eq. 5 clearly combines three branches (SA, CA, MSC). This contradicts itself — it should say 'three branches'. Minor but a genuine text/formula mismatch.

comments.title: fMSC described as 'multi scale attention' rather than multi scale context
comments.quote: _fMSC_ represents the multi scale attention
comments.explanation: Throughout the paper, MSC is defined as the Multi Scale Context module (summary, Section 4.3). Here in the aggregation description, it is called 'multi scale attention'. This is a minor terminology inconsistency that could mildly confuse readers about whether MSC is an attention-like module or a context module.

comments.title: Channel attention module described as extracting 'multi-scale' information without justification
comments.quote: The channel attention module is used for extracting high level multi-scale semantic information.
comments.explanation: The summary defines CA simply as extracting high-level semantic information. The CA module as formally defined uses global average pooling and two 1×1 convolutions — a standard SE-style block — which is not inherently multi-scale. Describing it as 'multi-scale' in this passage seems to overstate what the module does; the multi-scale aspect is supposed to be in the MSC module. Minor overclaim / terminology slippage.

comments.title: Cross-entropy loss formulation described in an unusual/incorrect way
comments.quote: where _N_ denotes the total number of samples, _yn_ denotes the probability that the forward propagation result is true, and 1- _yn_ denotes the probability that the forward propagation result is false.
comments.explanation: Standard cross-entropy for classification is -sum(y_true * log(y_pred)). Here the description calls y_n 'the probability that the forward propagation result is true', which conflates ground truth and prediction. Without the actual equation visible, I can't be certain, but this prose description is misleading and doesn't match the standard cross-entropy definition used in semantic segmentation (per-pixel multi-class CE). A careful reader would be confused about whether this is binary or multi-class CE, especially given multi-class segmentation (C classes).

comments.title: Duplicated SFNet rows in CamVid table
comments.explanation: Two pairs of identical SFNet rows appear in Table 2. This is almost certainly a copy-paste error in the table. Not affecting the main result but it's a genuine table error that should be flagged for correction.

comments.title: Ablation mIoU values inconsistent with main results and Table 6 column likely mislabeled
comments.explanation: The main results table (Table 1) reports AASeg achieving 74.8% mIoU on the Cityscapes validation set. However, the ablation tables (Tables 4, 5, 6) report the baseline/default AASeg configuration achieving 79.2% mIoU on the same Cityscapes validation set — a 4.4 percentage point discrepancy with no explanation. Additionally, in Table 6, the FPS value of 118.2 for the baseline matches the GFlops value for k=1 in Table 5, and the actual FPS reported for AASeg in Table 1 is 202.7. This suggests the 'FPS' column in Table 6 is actually GFLOPs (mislabeled). Either way this is a concrete inconsistency that undermines the interpretability of the ablation study.

comments.title: Dilation rate sequence inconsistent between architecture and ablation
comments.quote: We use increasing sequence of dilation rates in our network, which is 0, 1, 2, 3.
comments.explanation: Section 4.3 (MSC module description) specifies that the network uses 'dilated convolution layers with increasing dilation rates of 3, 6 and 12.' Here in the ablation, the text says the dilation rates are '0, 1, 2, 3' — a completely different sequence. Furthermore, Table 6 lists configurations r=1, r=2, r=3 (single rates) rather than a sequence. A dilation rate of 0 is also not well-defined (dilation rate 1 is standard convolution). This contradicts the earlier architecture description and leaves the reader unable to determine what dilation configuration is actually being evaluated.

comments.title: Two SFNet entries without distinguishing configuration
comments.explanation: Table 3 lists two SFNet rows with different performance numbers but no backbone or configuration indicator to distinguish them (unlike Table 2 where SFNet rows are labeled DF2 vs ResNet-18). A reader cannot determine which SFNet variant is which, which weakens the comparison.

The related work section reads as a disjointed list of one-sentence summaries without a coherent narrative or clear positioning of the contribution relative to prior art. Second, the writing quality is poor, with numerous grammatical errors, typos ("dosen't," "recpetive," "at at"), inconsistent citation formatting, and awkward phrasing throughout, which hinders comprehension. comments.title: Feature map dimensions omit channel separator
comments.quote: _Fl ∈ R[H][l][W][l][C][l]_ is a _Cl_ -dimensional feature map
comments.explanation: Initially I was concerned this might be inconsistent with the earlier definition X ∈ R^{H×W×3} and Y ∈ R^{H×W×C}, which use × between dimensions. Here F_l is written as R^{H_l W_l C_l} without the × separators. However, this is almost certainly an OCR/typesetting artifact rather than a real author error — the meaning is unambiguous (a feature map of spatial size H_l × W_l with C_l channels). Per instructions, I should not flag OCR artifacts that would not be a real issue assuming the most plausible intended symbol. Flagging as minor only because the inconsistency is visible in the passage.

comments.title: Convolution equation referenced but not shown
comments.quote:
We express a convolution layer _W[n]_ ( _x_ ) as follows:

where _⊙_ represents the convolution operator
comments.explanation: The text introduces a convolution layer W^n(x) 'as follows' but the actual equation does not appear in the extracted passage — only the 'where' clause defining the symbols is present. This could be an OCR extraction failure (the equation likely exists in the PDF). Since the instructions say not to flag incomplete text at passage boundaries or missing equations that exist elsewhere, this may simply be an extraction gap. However, the explanation defines ⊙ as the convolution operator and W^{n×n} as an n×n kernel, which is standard. No real issue assuming the equation is present in the original PDF.

comments.title: MSC module: channel reduction from 2048 is unexplained given no backbone
comments.quote: The output fused feature map is first convolved with a 1 _×_ 1 convolution to reduce the number of channels from 2048 to 256.
comments.explanation: The paper explicitly states (summary and Section 4.2) that AASeg does not use a backbone and that the input is passed through a conv+BN+ReLU block. It is unclear how the feature map at this stage reaches 2048 channels. Standard backbones like ResNet-50/101 produce 2048-channel features at their last stage, which suggests the authors may be reusing text from a backbone-based pipeline. Without specifying how the 2048-channel feature arises from their backbone-free architecture, this step is underspecified and raises doubts about the claim of not using a backbone.

comments.title: MSC module dilation rates: text inconsistency between description and branches
comments.quote: The feature map after passing through convolutional block is split to three different parts with 1 _×_ 1 convolution, 3 _×_ 3 convolution and 5 _×_ 5 convolution respectively.... The input feature map is convolved with dilated convolution layers with increasing dilation rates of 3, 6 and 12.
comments.explanation: Initially I read these as two complementary descriptions (multi-kernel branches followed by dilated convolutions). However, it's unclear how the two operations connect: are the 1×1/3×3/5×5 branches separate from the dilated convolutions with rates 3/6/12, or are the dilated convolutions applied to the fused output? Also, the ablation in Table 6 uses dilation rates r=1, 2, 3 (not 3, 6, 12), which contradicts the architecture description. The paper does not reconcile these values, making the MSC module's actual configuration ambiguous and the ablation's relationship to the main model unclear.

comments.title: Aggregation text refers to 'two branches' while formula has three
comments.quote: where _⊕_ represents the concatenation operator and _x_ 1, _x_ 2 and _x_ 3 represents the features of the two branches.
comments.explanation: The prose says 'two branches' but lists three variables x1, x2, x3, and the AASeg aggregation in Eq. 5 clearly combines three branches (SA, CA, MSC). This contradicts itself — it should say 'three branches'. Minor but a genuine text/formula mismatch.

comments.title: fMSC described as 'multi scale attention' rather than multi scale context
comments.quote: _fMSC_ represents the multi scale attention
comments.explanation: Throughout the paper, MSC is defined as the Multi Scale Context module (summary, Section 4.3). Here in the aggregation description, it is called 'multi scale attention'. This is a minor terminology inconsistency that could mildly confuse readers about whether MSC is an attention-like module or a context module.

comments.title: Ablation mIoU (~79%) inconsistent with reported Cityscapes val mIoU (74.8%)
comments.explanation: Table 1 reports AASeg achieving 74.8% mIoU on Cityscapes validation, but the ablation Tables 4, 5, and 6 report baseline/method values around 78–80% mIoU, also on Cityscapes validation. I considered whether these might refer to different training regimes, but no explanation is given. The ~5 percentage point gap between the reported main result and the ablation baselines is large and unexplained, which undermines the interpretability of the ablations and possibly the main result.

comments.title: Channel attention module described as extracting 'multi-scale' information without justification
comments.quote: The channel attention module is used for extracting high level multi-scale semantic information.
comments.explanation: The summary defines CA simply as extracting high-level semantic information. The CA module as formally defined uses global average pooling and two 1×1 convolutions — a standard SE-style block — which is not inherently multi-scale. Describing it as 'multi-scale' in this passage seems to overstate what the module does; the multi-scale aspect is supposed to be in the MSC module. Minor overclaim / terminology slippage.

comments.title: Cross-entropy loss formulation described in an unusual/incorrect way
comments.quote: where _N_ denotes the total number of samples, _yn_ denotes the probability that the forward propagation result is true, and 1- _yn_ denotes the probability that the forward propagation result is false.
comments.explanation: Standard cross-entropy for classification is -sum(y_true * log(y_pred)). Here the description calls y_n 'the probability that the forward propagation result is true', which conflates ground truth and prediction. Without the actual equation visible, I can't be certain, but this prose description is misleading and doesn't match the standard cross-entropy definition used in semantic segmentation (per-pixel multi-class CE). A careful reader would be confused about whether this is binary or multi-class CE, especially given multi-class segmentation (C classes).

comments.title: Duplicated SFNet rows in CamVid table
comments.explanation: Two pairs of identical SFNet rows appear in Table 2. This is almost certainly a copy-paste error in the table. Not affecting the main result but it's a genuine table error that should be flagged for correction.

comments.title: Ablation mIoU values inconsistent with main results
comments.explanation: The main results table (Table 1) reports AASeg achieving 74.8% mIoU on the Cityscapes validation set. However, the ablation tables (Tables 4, 5, 6) report the baseline/default AASeg configuration achieving 79.2% mIoU on the same Cityscapes validation set. This is a 4.4 percentage point discrepancy with no explanation. Either the ablation study uses a different configuration (not the one reported as the headline result), a different evaluation protocol, or there is an error. Also, in Table 6, the FPS value of 118.2 for the baseline is listed under an 'FPS' column, but Table 5 uses the same number 118.2 under 'GFlops' for k=1. This suggests the columns may be mislabeled in Table 6 (118.2 is likely GFLOPs, not FPS, given the main paper reports 202.7 FPS for AASeg). This inconsistency undermines the interpretability of the ablation study.

comments.title: Dilation rate sequence inconsistent between architecture and ablation
comments.quote: We use increasing sequence of dilation rates in our network, which is 0, 1, 2, 3.
comments.explanation: Section 4.3 (MSC module description) specifies that the network uses 'dilated convolution layers with increasing dilation rates of 3, 6 and 12.' Here in the ablation, the text says the dilation rates are '0, 1, 2, 3' — a completely different sequence. Furthermore, Table 6 lists configurations r=1, r=2, r=3 (single rates) rather than a sequence. A dilation rate of 0 is also not well-defined (dilation rate 1 is standard convolution). This contradicts the earlier architecture description and leaves the reader unable to determine what dilation configuration is actually being evaluated.

comments.title: Table 6 column labels likely swapped
comments.explanation: In Table 6, the column labeled 'FPS' shows values (118.2, 121.3, 115.5, 102.7, 88.6) that are in the same range as GFLOPs values in Table 5 (118.2, 120.8, 127.5, 136.1). The actual FPS reported for AASeg in Table 1 is 202.7. It appears the 'FPS' column in Table 6 is actually GFLOPs, and the table is missing an actual FPS column. Alternatively the labels are correct but then the FPS numbers dramatically contradict Table 1's headline claim of 202.7 FPS. Either way this is a concrete inconsistency that should be resolved.

comments.title: Two SFNet entries without distinguishing configuration
comments.explanation: Table 3 lists two SFNet rows with different performance numbers but no backbone or configuration indicator to distinguish them (unlike Table 2 where SFNet rows are labeled DF2 vs ResNet-18). A reader cannot determine which SFNet variant is which, which weakens the comparison.

progressive__gpt-5.5.label: Progressive (gpt-5.5)
progressive__gpt-5.5.overall_feedback: The paper addresses an important problem—real-time semantic segmentation—and the reported Cityscapes accuracy/FPS tradeoff is potentially interesting, but the current manuscript has substantial clarity, rigor, and originality concerns. The writing is often imprecise and grammatically weak, with many sentences that are difficult to parse, repeated typos, and several unsupported or incorrect claims about prior work; the related work is mostly a list of citations rather than a critical positioning of the proposed method. The claimed novelty is also unclear: spatial attention, channel attention, and multi-scale context modules are well-established components, so the paper needs to explain what is technically new in AASeg beyond combining existing ideas. The method description is underdeveloped in the provided sections: key architectural details, tensor shapes, module designs, training protocol, loss functions, and computational costs are missing or only vaguely described, and even the displayed convolution equation appears incomplete. The organization is also confusing, with datasets introduced inside the “Proposed Method” section and background material overlapping heavily with related work. Most importantly, the very strong speed claim of 202.7 FPS requires careful experimental specification—hardware, input resolution, batch size, implementation framework, timing protocol, and comparison fairness—otherwise the accuracy-speed comparison is not convincing. Overall, while the topic and empirical goal are relevant, the paper in its current form would need significantly clearer exposition, a stronger demonstration of novelty, and much more rigorous experimental methodology to be competitive at ICLR.

comments.title: Ambiguous claim that the model has “no backbone”
comments.quote: Our network dosen’t use any backbone to extract features from the input image unlike many previous architectures. The input image is first passed through a block comprising of convolutional, batch normalization and ReLU activation function.
comments.explanation: The likely intent is that AASeg does not use a standard pretrained classification backbone such as ResNet, Xception, or DFNet. That would be a meaningful point for real-time segmentation and is consistent with later comparison tables marking AASeg as having backbone “no.” However, taken literally, the statement is misleading because the described convolutional stages are themselves a feature extractor. The paper should clarify whether “no backbone” means no ImageNet-pretrained backbone, no external named backbone, or no separate encoder. This matters for interpreting speed/accuracy comparisons and pretraining assumptions.

comments.title: Core architecture and MSC channel dimensions are underspecified for reproduction
comments.quote: The input image X is converted to a set of feature maps Fl where l=1,...,3 from each network stage, where Fl ∈ R[H][l][W][l][C][l] is a Cl-dimensional feature map. ... The output fused feature map is first convolved with a 1 × 1 convolution to reduce the number of channels from 2048 to 256.
comments.explanation: The method describes three feature levels and later modules at a high level, but it does not specify enough architectural detail to reproduce the model: number of convolutional blocks per stage, channel widths Cl, strides/downsampling schedule, kernel sizes, padding, and the resolutions of the three feature maps before concatenation. The MSC description also states that a fused feature map is reduced from 2048 to 256 channels, but the paper does not explain where the 2048 channels come from, especially given that no external backbone is used and the branch output channel counts are not specified. These omissions affect parameters, FLOPs, and reproducibility.

comments.title: MSC role and aggregation are described inconsistently
comments.quote: Multi Scale Context(MSC) module to learn the information flow between feature maps of consecutive levels. ... xAASeg = ((fSA(xconcat) ⊗ xconcat) ⊕ (fCA(xconcat) ⊗ xconcat) ⊕ (fMSC(xconcat) ⊗ xconcat)) ... where ⊕ represents the concatenation operator and x1, x2 and x3 represents the features of the two branches.
comments.explanation: The paper first describes MSC as learning information flow between feature maps of consecutive levels, which suggests explicit cross-level feature-pyramid fusion. The later MSC description instead sounds like dense multi-kernel and dilated convolutions inside a module. The aggregation equation then treats fMSC(xconcat) like an attention mask multiplied by xconcat, whereas MSC was introduced as a context feature-producing module rather than a gate. This may be dimensionally invalid unless the MSC output is explicitly projected to the same shape and intended as a mask. The same aggregation section also refers to x1, x2, and x3 as features of “the two branches,” contradicting the three-module architecture. Overall, the role and tensor shapes of MSC at fusion time are ambiguous.

comments.title: Performance claim is underspecified: accuracy, speed, or Pareto tradeoff?
comments.quote: Our network performs better than most previous architectures with a 74.4% Mean IOU on Cityscapes test dataset while running at 202.7 FPS.
comments.explanation: The reported numbers may support a strong speed/accuracy tradeoff, but the phrase “performs better” is ambiguous. If it refers to mIoU alone, later results list several prior methods with higher Cityscapes test mIoU. If it refers to a tradeoff between mIoU and FPS, the paper should state the criterion explicitly. As written, the abstract could be read as claiming accuracy superiority over most prior work, when the defensible claim appears to be high speed with competitive accuracy.

comments.title: Related-work description appears to attribute channel-selection behavior to SqueezeNet
comments.quote: (Iandola et al., 2016) allows the neural network to find the critical channels of the feature map and select the most suitable channels by itself.
comments.explanation: Iandola et al. (2016) is SqueezeNet, whose main contribution is parameter reduction using Fire modules with squeeze and expand convolutions. It is not normally described as learning channel attention or selecting critical feature-map channels by itself. That description is closer to Squeeze-and-Excitation Networks, which are cited separately later. This should be corrected or rephrased to avoid confusing the lineage of the proposed channel-attention component.

comments.title: MSC dilation-rate definitions and ablation interpretation are inconsistent
comments.quote: The input feature map is convolved with dilated convolution layers with increasing dilation rates of 3, 6 and 12. ... We use increasing sequence of dilation rates in our network, which is 0, 1, 2, 3. ... It seems large dilation rates increases the performance but comes at the cost of increased number of parameters, hence decrease in FPS.
comments.explanation: The method section says the MSC uses dilation rates 3, 6, and 12, while the ablation section describes rates 0, 1, 2, and 3 and reports variants AASeg-(r=1), AASeg-(r=2), and AASeg-(r=3). It is unclear whether r denotes the actual dilation rate, a multiplier, the number of dilated stages, or an ablation index. The use of dilation rate 0 is also nonstandard, since dilation=1 is normally the non-dilated case. In addition, the paper claims that larger dilation rates increase parameter count, but changing dilation alone does not change the number of learned weights for fixed kernels and channels. The reported parameter increases therefore imply that something else changes across variants, but this is not explained.

comments.title: Cross-entropy loss is described as binary despite a multi-class segmentation setting
comments.quote: where N denotes the total number of samples, yn denotes the probability that the forward propagation result is true, and 1-yn denotes the probability that the forward propagation result is false.
comments.explanation: Cross-entropy is standard for semantic segmentation, and later notation refers to K categories. However, this explanation describes a binary true/false probability rather than the usual pixel-wise multi-class softmax cross-entropy over classes. If binary cross-entropy was used per class, that should be stated. If standard categorical cross-entropy was used, the notation and explanation should reflect per-pixel class labels and predicted class probabilities.

comments.title: Class attention loss appears inconsistent with the channel attention module’s stated structure
comments.quote: The class attention loss Lcls from channel attention module is also used. ... where aijk is the value generated of the class attention map of the jth pixel in the ith sample for the kth class.
comments.explanation: The CA module is described as using global average pooling and 1×1 convolutions, which conventionally produces channel-wise weights, not a dense per-pixel, per-class attention map aijk. The paper does not specify how this class attention map is produced, what spatial resolution it has, whether it is supervised with the segmentation ground truth, or how it connects to the channel attention vector. This makes the auxiliary loss difficult to reproduce or interpret.

comments.title: Validation and test training protocol for Cityscapes is ambiguous
comments.quote: We present the segmentation accuracy and inference speed of our proposed method on Cityscapes validation and test set in Table 1. We use the training set and validation set to train our models before submitting to Cityscapes online server.
comments.explanation: Training on train+val before submitting to the hidden Cityscapes test server is standard. However, the paper presents validation and test results together without clearly stating whether the validation mIoU was obtained from a model trained only on the training split, while the test result came from a train+val model. If the validation set was included in training for the model used to report validation mIoU, the validation result would not be a held-out estimate. The evaluation protocol should be made explicit.

comments.title: Efficiency comparisons lack sufficient protocol details for fair comparison
comments.quote: For quantitative evaluation of the performance of our network, mean of class-wise intersection-overunion (mIoU) is used for bench-marking the performance, number of float-point operations (FLOPs) and frames per second (FPS) are used for bench-marking the speed. ... Table 3: Performance comparison with results reported on ADE20K validation set.
comments.explanation: mIoU, FLOPs, and FPS are appropriate metrics, but the paper does not provide enough information to interpret the efficiency comparisons. For FPS, it does not specify GPU model, batch size, precision, warm-up, whether preprocessing/data transfer are included, or whether baselines were remeasured under the same setup or copied from original papers. Table 1 also compares methods at different input resolutions. The ADE20K GFLOPs table likewise omits input resolution, crop size, output stride, backbone/variant labels, and evaluation settings; two SFNet rows have different numbers but the same method name. Since FLOPs and FPS are highly setting-dependent, these omissions make the claimed efficiency advantage difficult to verify.

comments.title: Cityscapes validation mIoU in ablation tables conflicts with the main result
comments.quote: |AASeg|512×1024|no|74.8|74.4|202.7| ... Table 4: Ablation study on Upsampling operation in our network using Cityscapes validation set. ... |bilinear upsampling|79.2| ... Table 6 reports AASeg variants up to 80.2 mIoU.
comments.explanation: The main Cityscapes table reports AASeg validation mIoU as 74.8%, but later ablation tables on the Cityscapes validation set report values around 78.3–80.2%. This is too large to be explained as ordinary variation and reverses the relationship between the reported final model and its ablated variants. The discrepancy could be due to a different training schedule, crop/scale evaluation, validation protocol, class set, or stronger model variant, but none of that is stated. As written, it is unclear which model and protocol produced the headline Cityscapes numbers.

comments.title: Ablation FPS values are inconsistent with the headline AASeg speed at the same resolution
comments.quote: Table 6: Ablation study results on Cityscapes validation set. FPS are estimated for an input image of resolution of 512 × 1024. ... AASeg-baseline: 118.2 FPS; AASeg-w/o dilation: 121.3 FPS; AASeg-(r=3): 88.6 FPS.
comments.explanation: The main comparison table reports AASeg at 512×1024 with 202.7 FPS, but the ablation table reports AASeg-related variants at the same stated resolution with only 88.6–121.3 FPS. The text does not explain which ablation variant corresponds to the final architecture or why the headline model is much faster. Because the paper’s central claim depends on the speed/accuracy tradeoff, this internal inconsistency substantially weakens confidence in the reported speed numbers.

comments.title: CamVid comparison table contains duplicated SFNet entries
comments.explanation: The CamVid table repeats the same two SFNet rows. This is likely a table-preparation error and may not change the numerical conclusion, but duplicated baseline rows create confusion about which comparisons were actually included and suggest the table was not carefully checked.

comments.title: Kernel-size ablation marks the less efficient tied result as uniquely best
comments.explanation: The kernel-size ablation reports the same mIoU for k=5 and k=7, but k=7 has higher GFLOPs. If the criterion is highest mIoU, both should be marked as tied. If the criterion is best accuracy/compute tradeoff, k=5 is preferable. Highlighting only k=7 as best can mislead readers about what the ablation supports.

comments.title: “Without adding extra computational cost” overstates what SA and CA modules can do
comments.quote: We use Spatial Attention(SA) and Channel Attention(CA) modules to enhance features of objects without adding extra computational cost.
comments.explanation: The attention modules may be lightweight, but operations such as 1×1 convolutions, pooling, sigmoid activations, and element-wise multiplication still add parameters, memory access, and FLOPs relative to omitting the modules. Unless the paper shows that these costs are exactly offset elsewhere, the claim should be softened to say that the modules add modest or negligible overhead rather than no computational cost.

progressive_original__gpt-5.5.label: Progressive Original (gpt-5.5)
progressive_original__gpt-5.5.overall_feedback: The paper addresses an important problem—real-time semantic segmentation—and the reported Cityscapes accuracy/FPS tradeoff is potentially interesting, but the current manuscript has substantial clarity, rigor, and originality concerns. The writing is often imprecise and grammatically weak, with many sentences that are difficult to parse, repeated typos, and several unsupported or incorrect claims about prior work; the related work is mostly a list of citations rather than a critical positioning of the proposed method. The claimed novelty is also unclear: spatial attention, channel attention, and multi-scale context modules are well-established components, so the paper needs to explain what is technically new in AASeg beyond combining existing ideas. The method description is underdeveloped in the provided sections: key architectural details, tensor shapes, module designs, training protocol, loss functions, and computational costs are missing or only vaguely described, and even the displayed convolution equation appears incomplete. The organization is also confusing, with datasets introduced inside the “Proposed Method” section and background material overlapping heavily with related work. Most importantly, the very strong speed claim of 202.7 FPS requires careful experimental specification—hardware, input resolution, batch size, implementation framework, timing protocol, and comparison fairness—otherwise the accuracy-speed comparison is not convincing. Overall, while the topic and empirical goal are relevant, the paper in its current form would need significantly clearer exposition, a stronger demonstration of novelty, and much more rigorous experimental methodology to be competitive at ICLR.

comments.title: Ambiguous claim that the model has “no backbone”
comments.quote: Our network dosen’t use any backbone to extract features from the input image unlike many previous architectures. The input image is first passed through a block comprising of convolutional, batch normalization and ReLU activation function.
comments.explanation: I understand the authors’ likely intent: they appear to mean that AASeg does not use a standard pretrained classification backbone such as ResNet, Xception, or DFNet. That would be a meaningful point for real-time segmentation, and it is consistent with the comparison tables later marking AASeg as having backbone “no.” However, taken literally, the statement is misleading: the described convolutional stages are themselves a feature-extraction backbone in the usual semantic-segmentation sense. The later context does not fully resolve whether “no backbone” means no ImageNet-pretrained backbone, no external named backbone, or no separate encoder at all. This matters because the speed/accuracy comparison depends on whether competing methods are being compared under the same assumptions about pretraining and feature extractors.

comments.title: Core architecture is underspecified for reproduction
comments.quote:
The input image _X_ is converted to a set of feature maps _Fl_ where l=1,...,3 from each network stage, where _Fl ∈ R[H][l][W][l][C][l]_ is a _Cl_ -dimensional feature map.

Our network dosen’t use any backbone to extract features from the input image unlike many previous architectures. The input image is first passed through a block comprising of convolutional, batch normalization and ReLU activation function.
comments.explanation: The high-level description makes clear that the model produces three levels of feature maps and then applies the proposed modules, so the overall intent is understandable. I also checked the later context, where Figure 4 is said to show the complete architecture and later sections define SA, CA, MSC, and aggregation. What remains problematic is that the text does not specify the essential architectural choices needed to reproduce the feature extractor: number of convolutional blocks per stage, channel widths C_l, strides/downsampling schedule, kernel sizes, padding, and whether the three feature maps have matched or different resolutions before concatenation. If these are only present visually in a figure, the written method is still fragile; if they are absent from the figure as well, the method is not reproducible from the paper.

comments.title: MSC contribution is described as cross-level fusion, but later context describes intra-module dense dilated convolutions
comments.quote: Multi Scale Context(MSC) module to learn the information flow between feature maps of consecutive levels.
comments.explanation: The phrase initially suggests a feature-pyramid-style operation that explicitly models information flow between consecutive network levels, e.g. between F_1, F_2, and F_3. I checked the later MSC description: it says the feature map is split through 1×1, 3×3, and 5×5 convolutions and then passed through dilated convolutions whose inputs are concatenations of the original feature map and previous convolution outputs. That sounds like dense connectivity among convolutional branches inside the MSC module, not necessarily information flow between consecutive feature hierarchy levels. The authors may be using “levels” informally to mean successive convolutional stages inside MSC, but a careful reader could instead expect an explicit inter-level fusion mechanism. The claim should be clarified or aligned with the formal module definition.

comments.title: Performance claim is underspecified: accuracy, speed, or Pareto tradeoff?
comments.quote: Our network performs better than most previous architectures with a 74.4% Mean IOU on Cityscapes test dataset while running at 202.7 FPS.
comments.explanation: The reported numbers are plausible in the context of the later Cityscapes table: AASeg is indeed faster than many listed methods and has competitive mIoU. However, “performs better” is ambiguous. If it refers to mIoU alone, later results show several prior methods with higher Cityscapes test mIoU, including BiSeNetV1-ResNet18, SFNet, BiSeNetV2-L, STDC1-Seg75, and STDC2-Seg75. If it refers to a speed/accuracy tradeoff, the claim is more defensible, but then the metric or Pareto criterion should be stated explicitly. As written, the abstract could lead readers to interpret 74.4% as accuracy-superior to most prior work, when the stronger point is really the combination of high FPS and competitive accuracy.

comments.title: Related-work description appears to attribute channel-selection behavior to SqueezeNet
comments.quote: (Iandola et al., 2016) allows the neural network to find the critical channels of the feature map and select the most suitable channels by itself.
comments.explanation: I considered whether this might be a loose summary of lightweight network design rather than an attention mechanism. But Iandola et al. (2016) is SqueezeNet, whose main contribution is parameter reduction using Fire modules with squeeze and expand convolutions; it is not normally described as learning channel attention or selecting critical feature-map channels by itself. That description is much closer to Squeeze-and-Excitation Networks, which are correctly cited later as Hu et al. (2018). This is localized to the related-work discussion, but it can confuse the lineage of the proposed channel-attention component and should be corrected or rephrased.

comments.title: MSC channel dimensions are not traceable from the described architecture
comments.quote: The output fused feature map is first convolved with a 1 _×_ 1 convolution to reduce the number of channels from 2048 to 256.
comments.explanation: I can see the intended design: the MSC module first gathers multi-kernel features and then uses a 1×1 projection to keep the representation lightweight, which is a standard and sensible pattern. However, I could not resolve where the value 2048 comes from. Earlier the paper only defines generic feature maps \(F_l \in \mathbb{R}^{H_l \times W_l \times C_l}\), and the authors emphasize that the model does not use an external backbone, so there is no obvious ResNet-like 2048-channel stage to appeal to. The three branches are described as 1×1, 3×3, and 5×5 convolutions, but their output channel counts are not specified, so a reader cannot derive the concatenated channel dimension. This is not merely a notation issue: the 2048→256 projection materially affects parameters, FLOPs, and reproducibility.

comments.title: MSC dilation rates conflict with the later ablation description
comments.quote: The input feature map is convolved with dilated convolution layers with increasing dilation rates of 3, 6 and 12.
comments.explanation: The use of dilated convolutions with rates 3, 6, and 12 is plausible and resembles ASPP-style context aggregation. However, later in the paper the ablation section states that the network uses an increasing sequence of dilation rates “0, 1, 2, 3” and reports variants AASeg-(r=1), AASeg-(r=2), and AASeg-(r=3). I considered whether these might refer to different concepts, e.g. maximum dilation stage versus absolute dilation rates, but the text does not distinguish them. As written, the reader cannot tell whether the actual MSC module uses rates \(3,6,12\), rates \(0,1,2,3\), or some other parameterization. This affects both reproduction of the model and interpretation of the ablation.

comments.title: MSC is treated as an attention mask in the aggregation equation, but described as a feature-producing context module
comments.quote: _xAASeg_ = (( _fSA_ ( _xconcat_ ) _⊗ xconcat_ ) _⊕_ ( _fCA_ ( _xconcat_ ) _⊗ xconcat_ ) _⊕_ ( _fMSC_ ( _xconcat_ ) _⊗ xconcat_ )) (5)
comments.explanation: The first two terms are understandable: spatial and channel attention modules often output masks that can be multiplied with the input feature tensor, with broadcasting where needed. The third term is harder to reconcile with the preceding MSC description. The MSC module is described as applying multi-kernel convolutions, channel reduction, dilated convolutions, and concatenation, i.e. as producing a transformed context feature map rather than an attention mask. Multiplying \(f_{MSC}(x_{concat})\) by \(x_{concat}\) may be dimensionally invalid unless the MSC output is explicitly projected to the same shape and intended as a gate. The following prose also calls \(f_{MSC}\) “multi scale attention,” whereas the module has been introduced as “Multi Scale Context.” This leaves the formal architecture ambiguous at a key fusion point.

comments.title: Concatenation definition says three inputs but describes two branches
comments.quote: where _⊕_ represents the concatenation operator and _x_ 1, _x_ 2 and _x_ 3 represents the features of the two branches.
comments.explanation: The intended operation is probably simple concatenation of three feature tensors, and the equation context later combines SA, CA, and MSC outputs. Still, the prose says \(x_1, x_2, x_3\) are features of “the two branches,” which contradicts the three listed tensors and the three-module architecture. This is likely a wording error rather than a conceptual flaw, but it can mislead a reader about whether the aggregation has two streams or three.

comments.title: Cross-entropy loss is described as binary despite a multi-class segmentation setting
comments.quote: where _N_ denotes the total number of samples, _yn_ denotes the probability that the forward propagation result is true, and 1- _yn_ denotes the probability that the forward propagation result is false.
comments.explanation: Using cross-entropy is standard for semantic segmentation, and the later auxiliary-loss notation correctly refers to \(K\) categories. However, this explanation of Equation 8 describes a binary true/false probability, not the usual pixel-wise multi-class softmax cross-entropy over \(C\) or \(K\) classes. I considered whether this could simply be an informal simplification, but the paper’s core task is explicitly multi-class semantic segmentation, and the loss definition is part of the reproducible method. If the authors actually used binary cross-entropy per class, that should be stated; if they used standard categorical cross-entropy, the notation and explanation should reflect per-pixel class labels and predicted class probabilities.

comments.title: Class attention loss appears inconsistent with the channel attention module’s stated structure
comments.quote:
The class attention loss _Lcls_ from channel attention module is also used. The class attention loss is

where _aijk_ is the value generated of the class attention map of the _j[th]_ pixel in the _i[th]_ sample for the _k[th]_ class.
comments.explanation: It is reasonable to add auxiliary supervision to encourage attention maps to align with semantic classes. The issue is that the CA module is described immediately above as using global average pooling and 1×1 convolutions, which conventionally produces channel-wise weights, not a dense per-pixel, per-class attention map \(a_{ijk}\). I checked whether the text defines an additional projection from channel attention weights to a class attention map, but that mechanism is not specified in the shown method. As written, it is unclear how \(a_{ijk}\) is obtained, what its spatial resolution is, whether it is supervised with the same ground truth as the segmentation logits, and how this loss connects to a channel attention vector.

comments.title: Validation and test training protocol for Cityscapes is ambiguous
comments.quote: We present the segmentation accuracy and inference speed of our proposed method on Cityscapes validation and test set in Table 1. We use the training set and validation set to train our models before submitting to Cityscapes online server.
comments.explanation: The second sentence matches the standard Cityscapes practice of training on train+val before submitting to the hidden test server. The concern is that the first sentence presents both validation and test results in the same table, but the paper does not clearly state whether the validation mIoU was obtained from a model trained only on the training split, while the test result was obtained from a train+val model. If the validation set was included in training for the model used to report validation mIoU, the validation number would not be a valid held-out estimate. This may be only a reporting ambiguity, but the current wording leaves a careful reader unsure about the evaluation protocol.

comments.title: FPS comparisons are not reproducible or clearly comparable across methods
comments.quote: For quantitative evaluation of the performance of our network, mean of class-wise intersection-overunion (mIoU) is used for bench-marking the performance, number of float-point operations (FLOPs) and frames per second (FPS) are used for bench-marking the speed.
comments.explanation: mIoU, FLOPs, and FPS are appropriate metrics for real-time segmentation, so the chosen metrics are reasonable. What remains unresolved is the measurement protocol for FPS, which is central to the paper’s main claim. The passage does not specify GPU model, batch size at inference, precision, warm-up, whether data transfer and preprocessing are included, or whether FPS values for baselines were remeasured under the same setup or copied from original papers. Table 1 also compares methods at different input resolutions, such as 512×1024, 768×1536, 1024×1024, and 1024×2048. Without a controlled protocol, the reported 202.7 FPS is difficult to interpret relative to the baselines.

comments.title: Cityscapes validation mIoU in the main table conflicts with later ablation numbers
comments.explanation: The table row itself is clear and reports a plausible real-time Cityscapes result. However, later ablation tables in the same paper report Cityscapes validation mIoUs of 79.2 for AASeg-baseline and up to 80.2 for AASeg-(r=3), also at 512×1024 for FPS estimation. I considered whether the ablation might use a different split, crop protocol, multi-scale testing, or a smaller validation subset, but no such distinction is stated. A five-point gap between the main AASeg validation result and the ablation variants is too large to ignore and changes the interpretation of both the headline result and the ablation study.

comments.title: CamVid comparison table contains duplicated SFNet entries
comments.explanation: The duplicated entries are easy to spot and probably arose during table preparation rather than from the method itself. Still, comparison tables are evidence for the empirical claim, and duplicated rows can create confusion about which baselines were actually considered. It also raises a mild concern that the table was not carefully checked, although the numerical conclusions for AASeg versus the listed SFNet variants are unchanged.

comments.title: Ablation mIoU values conflict with the main Cityscapes validation result
comments.quote:
Table 4: Ablation study on Upsampling operation in our network using Cityscapes validation set. Best results are highlighted in bold.

|Method|mIoU (%)|
|bilinear upsampling|**79.2**|
|deconvolution|78.5|
|nearest neighbor|78.3|
comments.explanation: I first read these as ordinary component ablations on the same Cityscapes validation split used earlier, and the caption explicitly says they are on the Cityscapes validation set. Checking against the earlier results table, however, the reported full AASeg validation mIoU is 74.8%, while all ablation values here are around 78.3–79.2%, and Table 6 goes as high as 80.2%. That is not a small experimental fluctuation; it reverses the relationship between the reported final model and its ablated variants. This could be resolved if the ablations used a different training schedule, validation protocol, crop/scale evaluation, class set, or a stronger variant than the submitted AASeg model, but none of that is stated. As written, the ablation section makes it unclear which model produced the headline Cityscapes numbers and undermines the interpretation of the reported improvements.

comments.title: Ablation FPS values are inconsistent with the headline AASeg speed at the same resolution
comments.quote:
Table 6: Ablation study results on Cityscapes validation set. FPS are estimated for an input image of resolution of 512 _×_ 1024.

|Model|mIoU (%)|FPS|Parameters (M)|
|---|---|---|---|
|AASeg-baseline|79.2|118.2|**0.76**|
|AASeg-w/o dilation|77.4|**121.3**|0.80|
|AASeg-(r=1)|79.8|115.5|0.79|
|AASeg-(r=2)|80.1|102.7|0.84|
|AASeg-(r=3)|**80.2**|88.6|0.90|
comments.explanation: The table is useful in principle because it reports FPS under a stated input resolution. But earlier, the main comparison table reports AASeg at 512 × 1024 with 202.7 FPS. Here, at the same stated resolution, the fastest AASeg-related variant is only 121.3 FPS, and the best-accuracy variant is 88.6 FPS. I considered whether “AASeg-baseline” might be a deliberately slower or different model than the final AASeg, but the table contains AASeg variants and the text does not explain which one corresponds to the final reported architecture. Since the paper’s central claim is a favorable speed/accuracy tradeoff, this unresolved discrepancy substantially weakens the reliability of the reported speed numbers.

comments.title: Dilation-rate description conflicts with the earlier MSC definition and uses a nonstandard rate 0
comments.quote: We use increasing sequence of dilation rates in our network, which is 0, 1, 2, 3.
comments.explanation: The authors clearly intend to study the effect of dilation, and Table 6 is organized around variants with different r values. However, earlier in the method section the MSC module is described as using dilation rates 3, 6, and 12. This passage instead says the sequence is 0, 1, 2, 3. In standard convolution implementations, dilation=1 is the ordinary non-dilated convolution, while dilation=0 is not a valid dilation rate. I considered whether this could be an OCR issue, but the surrounding Table 6 labels AASeg-(r=1), AASeg-(r=2), and AASeg-(r=3), so the ambiguity appears substantive rather than just a symbol misread. The paper needs to clarify whether r denotes the actual dilation rate, a multiplier, the number of dilated stages, or an ablation index; otherwise the MSC design is not reproducible.

comments.title: Claim that larger dilation rates increase parameter count is technically misleading
comments.quote: It seems large dilation rates increases the performance but comes at the cost of increased number of parameters, hence decrease in FPS.
comments.explanation: The qualitative trend in Table 6 is that the listed variants have higher mIoU and lower FPS as r increases, so the authors are trying to explain a real empirical trend. The problem is the stated mechanism: for a fixed convolution kernel size and fixed input/output channel counts, changing the dilation rate changes the sampling pattern and receptive field but does not change the number of learnable weights. The table does show parameter counts increasing from 0.79M to 0.90M across r=1 to r=3, but that cannot be caused by dilation rate alone under the standard definition of dilated convolution. This suggests either that additional layers/channels are being added in these variants, or that r means something other than dilation rate. Without that clarification, the ablation’s interpretation is technically incorrect or at least underspecified.

comments.title: ADE20K GFLOPs comparison omits key conditions needed for a fair efficiency comparison
comments.quote:
Table 3: Performance comparison with results reported on ADE20K validation set. Best results are highlighted in bold.

|Method|Mean IoU(%)|GFLOPs|
|---|---|---|
|PSPNet50|42.78|167.6|
|PSPNet101|43.29|238.4|
|SFNet|42.81|**75.7**|
|SFNet|44.67|94.0|
|DCANet|45.49|-|
|AASeg|**46.29**|80.26|
comments.explanation: The table is intended to show that AASeg is both accurate and computationally efficient on ADE20K. The mIoU comparison is straightforward, but the GFLOPs column is hard to interpret because FLOPs for semantic segmentation scale strongly with input resolution, crop size, output stride, and sometimes multi-scale testing conventions. Unlike the Cityscapes and CamVid tables, this table does not list resolution, backbone/variant, or evaluation setting. The two SFNet rows also have identical method names but different mIoU and GFLOPs, presumably corresponding to different backbones or variants, yet this is not identified. A careful reader therefore cannot tell whether AASeg’s 80.26 GFLOPs is being compared under the same input assumptions as PSPNet, SFNet, and DCANet. This does not necessarily invalidate the ADE20K result, but it makes the efficiency comparison underjustified.

comments.title: Kernel-size ablation marks the less efficient tied result as uniquely best
comments.explanation: The table usefully reports both accuracy and computational cost, and the accompanying text correctly notes that k=7 gives similar performance with higher complexity. However, k=5 and k=7 have the same reported mIoU, while k=5 uses fewer GFLOPs. If the criterion is highest mIoU, both should be tied; if the criterion is the best accuracy/compute tradeoff, k=5 is preferable to k=7. Highlighting only k=7 as the best can mislead the reader about which kernel size the ablation actually supports.

comments.title: “Without adding extra computational cost” overstates what SA and CA modules can do
comments.quote: We use Spatial Attention(SA) and Channel Attention(CA) modules to enhance features of objects without adding extra computational cost.
comments.explanation: The intended point seems to be that the attention modules are lightweight, which is plausible given the earlier definitions using 1 × 1 convolutions, pooling, sigmoid activations, and element-wise multiplication. But those operations still add parameters, memory access, and FLOPs relative to omitting the modules. Standard channel/spatial attention modules are often inexpensive, not free. Unless the authors have shown that these costs are exactly offset elsewhere in the architecture, the phrase “without adding extra computational cost” is too strong. A more defensible claim would be that the modules add negligible or modest overhead.
